sentences.text: Date is May 29, 1996.
The survivor is Ruth Minsky Sender.
The interview is taking place in Commack, New York.
The interviewer is Anne Bernard, and the interview was taking place in English.
I appreciate the fact you spelt my name with an e.
Date is May 29, 1996.
The survivor is Ruth Minsky Sender.
The interviewer is Anne Bernard.
The interview is taking place in Commack, New York, the United States of America.
And we're doing the interview in English.
Ruth Minsky Sender.
My Yiddish name is Riva.
Minska.
Lodz, Poland.
I just turned 70.
My mother's name was Nacha Grundman Minska.
My father's name was Avrom Minski.
My mother was born in Lodz.
My father was born outside Lodz in a town named Koluszki.
I had two sisters and four brothers.
My oldest was Mala.
She was six years older than I was.
My second sister is Chana, and she's four years older.
And my brother is Yankl, and he's three years older.
And I had tree younger sib-- siblings who did not survive.
And there was Motele, and he was two years younger.
And there was Laibele, and there was like two years between each, and Laibele was after Motele.
And then was Avrom Moishe, and he was born just after my father died, so his name was after my father and my grandfather.
My father was Avrom, so he was Avrom Moishe.
There was an epidemic, a typhus epidemic.
And everybody survived except him.
Yes.
She was a widow with seven children and kept them all at home.
Sent them to private Yiddish schools, ran her own business.
And as a child, maybe I didn't realize how much she struggled because she put up a big front and people thought that she was doing well.
We were dressed decent.
We went to private schools.
But my older sister always said she knew how hard she struggled, but she kept her children home.
It was a specialty manufacturing little girl's coats.
And usually little girls-- only very rich little girls could afford it, coats made to order.
So she was making those coats and selling it to the stores, manufacturing them and selling it to the stores.
And those were a-- it was specialty trade.
She had people working for her.
She did the designing, the cutting.
And she had people that did the sewing and the finishing and the pressing and the delivery to the stores.
No.
No.
They were too young.
Their first-- my brother sometimes would help with delivering the, the-- bringing whatever to other manufacturers, materials delivering.
My oldest sister went to work.
She was 12 years old when my father died, and she looked older, so she got working papers.
She took the birth certificate of a older cousin-- same name.
Esther was her name, so then she was called Esther and then Malka or Mala.
And she went to work in a factory at age 12, actually.
Supposed to be that she was 16.
And she worked all her life to help my mother until she was-- she left Poland when the war started in 1939.
My other sister, when she finished school she went to a trade school.
I don't remember exactly what she was learning in that trade school.
It was an art school, where they were teaching all different-- they still do.
Or it still is active teaching people.
And my brother was still in school.
I was still in school, so the rest of them, so they really did not help my mother as far as helping, except that my oldest sister worked and helped earning money.
He worked with my mother, and he was a freelance newspaper writer.
He was-- actually, he wrote satire.
And it's interesting.
He would make fun of the political parties of the Germans coming to power.
We already knew at that time-- Hitler, you know-- it was only '31, but we knew that what Hitler's plans when he wrote And, and my mother collected all the newspapers that he wrote.
She put them in big-- not albums, like scrapbooks, but huge scrapbooks.
And then when the Germans came in 1939, she had to rip it up and burn it because she was afraid that they're going to see that he, he wrote against it.
Though it was in Yiddish, you see, I remember her burning those books.
We asked why she's doing it.
But we understood why she's doing it.
She was afraid it would endanger us.
We had a two room apartment, rented.
It wasn't-- we lived in-- after my father died, because when my father died, I was five years old.
So I really don't remember much.
We moved in with our grandmother, my mother's mother.
We have one of the pictures still.
It was a two room apartment that we shared, shared together.
My mother's factory was in a different place.
So-- and then we had a housekeeper nanny member of our family.
Her name was Antosia.
We called her Tosia.
She was a peasant girl, but she spoke Yiddish, like everybody spoke Yiddish over there.
And she stayed with us all the time, so she was the other member of the family until the ghetto.
In the ghetto, she insisted to staying with us, but she would have been arrested, and we would have been punished.
So she had to leave.
Yeah, yes.
She lived with us.
She lived with us.
Usually three people in a bed.
The room-- the second room had I think three beds.
Yeah, there was two beds-- four beds, actually.
And then there was something in that first room that opened up.
In the daytime, it looked like a break front or something.
And when-- there was a bottom drawer.
You opened it up and that's where a bed-- where you slept.
No.
We had to go outside, and there was a water pump that you carried water b-- and we lived in a big city.
It's not that the big city didn't have running water.
We just didn't.
Only the more affluent sections of the city had running water and had toilets.
We had to go outside a water pump and bring it in buckets and keep it in the house.
And there was outside toilets, an outhouse.
And we would-- at night, we would have buckets in the house because you didn't want to run at night to this.
My aunt, which lived in better section in the city of Lodz, had running water.
They had plumbing.
They had showers.
They had-- so depending on the money, if you could afford it to live in the areas that had the-- we had electricity.
We weren't that poor.
But we had electricity.
Before that where my father-- where we lived before my father died-- we lived on [NON-ENGLISH] 11.
I'm trying to think Yiddish translate to English.
[LAUGHS] And I remembered a two room apartment.
And two of them worked from the house in that apartment, ran the business from there.
And we had a cousin living next door.
Not much else that I remember.
But I just remember when we moved when my father died.
My mother had to move out because it was too expensive already to stay there, so she moved in with our grandmother.
And that was [NON-ENGLISH].
I'm giving it in English.
[NON-ENGLISH], how would it be in English?
Well, that would-- the name would be the same, [NON-ENGLISH] 60.
And that's where we lived with the grandma until she died in 1939.
What she died of?
I know my sister kept telling me that she had throat cancer.
I was young, and I just didn't pay much attention.
Grandma was always there and suddenly she was-- she was gone.
But my sister had said-- because she was six years older.
And those six years seem to have made a big difference because she was married at 19.
And you know, so there's especially a big difference.
She's the married woman and here's that, that little kid.
So she, she knew much more than I did.
And she just died January 6 of this year.
Not really.
Not really.
That was so wonderful with our mother because she seemed to make-- she had seven children, and each one felt like they're the favorite.
I don't know how she did it, but each child felt that they are.
They're the special child.
They're the favorite child.
And she did her very best.
She had an uncle of mine, her younger brother, that she had actually put to school.
He became-- he wanted to become a doctor.
But to become a doctor in Poland, because of anti-Semitism, were so many restrictions.
He became a teacher and later taught in the school that I attended.
And he paid my tuition.
And he helped her.
I remember seeing also-- as a child, somehow you don't pay attention.
But I remember sometimes him, him coming and he giving her money.
She felt uncomfortable.
So those scenes, I remember.
So he helped the money-wise later.
But somehow we-- people thought that we were rich.
I don't know how she put up that big front, but she did.
Holidays.
Her sitting and singing Yiddish songs.
In fact, in one of my books I translated a song in English, you know?
Most of times when she had all the children together and made us all feel special.
[YIDDISH], spring is coming, and the holiday of Pesach is coming.
And tell me, children, what the holiday is bringing.
What-- and it comes with nuts and it comes with candies and this is the holiday of spring.
It was a famous Yiddish folk song.
A lot of songs she always used to sing to us in Yiddish.
At our home in general, we did a lot of singing and talking and a lot of people were always there, so it--
Did she cook a lot for the children?
Most of the cooking was done by Tosia-- Antosia.
Her short name was Tosia.
For Shabbas, my mother used to do the cooking.
I remember baking.
I remember she-- in fact, to this day, I have a recipe, an apple cake.
And you know how European woman said a little bit of this, a little bit of that-- no recipe.
So one day my sister-- my daughter said to her, so hold it.
Before you put that little [INAUDIBLE] in a little [INAUDIBLE], this is this, and this is this.
And now we have it written down, Grandma's Apple Cake.
It is-- I remember baking, baking a lot.
And when she came Friday, quickly before, you know, because she had to work, she would still bake.
And Saturday morning, we all had cake.
We might not have bread, but we had cake.
[LAUGHS] Yeah, she-- baking, she did a lot of baking.
She did cooking too, but every day cooking-- for the holidays or whatever.
Everyday cooking was the Tosia.
Tosia did the shopping and the cooking and taking in children and running to my mother, telling what the problems are.
She was so involved in our life.
She would complain to my mother.
My sister told me that, that at that time-- and somebody just told me in Israel, they're still doing it.
They have the little grocery store where you buy on credit.
They write down what you buy, and when you have the money, you pay.
Well, my mother did it because she had to wait for the season that she would get paid.
You know, this is a seasonal manufacturing business that she was running.
So she always paid her employees first.
And for herself, she waited.
So Tosia would always say, you're sending the children to that grocery store, and this guy, watch that he pads-- because I'm trying to think the Yiddish to English translation-- he pads your books.
He writes things that they didn't buy.
He exaggerates.
Said, well, I have no choice.
But she would get upset that he's-- you know.
And she was so careful with my mother's money.
She would go to the-- she knew what the season when the money, when there is no money.
So if there was no money, she would buy little rolls-- the darker bread.
You know, kind of a mixture of pumpernickel-- because they were cheaper.
And she would kind of take cocoa and add water because she kind of tried to stretch the money that was coming to us.
She was a member of the family.
No.
No.
She, she was a peasant girl, but she came to us at a young age.
I never saw any, any signs of anti-Semitism.
And she spoke Yiddish, and she knew the customs.
And I think she kind of grew up with us.
She was a little bit older than my-- than my older sister, you know?
No, I knew that she wasn't Jewish because sometimes she would talk about her family.
I don't know where they lived.
No, we knew that she wasn't Jewish.
I attended a Jewish day school, which was a luxury because the Jewish schools, because of anti-Semitism, had the-- it was expected much more often than the public school.
There were always inspectors coming to the schools and check our educational level, how far we whatever.
We always had to be above the public school because they were looking for excuse to close up those schools.
So we really would receive a wonderful education in those Yiddish private schools.
I was-- I was a member at several libraries.
I guess there weren't enough books for me to read.
I always loved to read.
I still do.
We belonged to clubs, to socialist groups.
We belonged to sport clubs, spend a lot of times with teenagers, like in here-- does he love me?
Does he not?
Is he going to ask-- well, we didn't go out on dates.
We always went on groups.
But we were in the same social problems.
Does he or doesn't he?
Does he-- is he going to talk to me?
Is he going to ask me?
Do you think he likes me?
Sometimes when I watch teenagers, and I say, things really didn't change much.
Now they have telephones and whatever the new gadgets to communicate.
And we did it-- orally, we communicated.
We spent a lot of times talking and going for walks and making a lot of noise in the streets.
Did you go-- did you go with boys at an early age?
Not singly.
Always in groups.
Boys and girls in groups, you know?
Though you had one favorite guy, and you hoped that he likes you too.
But very seldom that we would go out on the own, like dating in here.
Now you wouldn't start dating until you were really older.
Literature, Yiddish literature, Polish literature.
I didn't like math too much.
I mean, I had to do it.
I had to do it.
But literature because I liked stories.
I liked things becoming alive.
I could put myself in there.
I liked poetry.
And there was one time-- I was a bashful child, so I really couldn't show that I'm a good student because I wouldn't raise my hand unless the teacher called on me.
So now as a teacher, I appreciate it.
And one-- we had a lot of assignments, poetry.
And I think it was wonderful because to this day, I remember a lot of poems in Yiddish and Polish.
I went to a school over here, and I talked about a Polish poet and his poem that meant to-- where he compared good health to freedom.
And I didn't really appreciate it because I want to bring it in the value of freedom.
Afterwards, teacher-- the principal told me there's a little girl from Poland.
She's 14.
He said, do you know the poem Mrs.
Sender had talked about?
She said, yes.
And we both started reciting the poem.
And now here, it's been so many years since I recited the poem, so I think it was good learning all, all those poems.
Can you recite it for us?
[SPEAKING POLISH].
It means, the one that lost you.
Of course, he was talking about Polish freedom, that they lost the Polish freedom.
And the one that could appreciate you is the one that lost you.
And we had a poem in school-- we often-- Yiddish and Polish poetry.
And the teacher would call to recite the poem.
But this was the homework assignment.
I would never volunteer.
So one day when she called me-- because, you know, it's time that you say some-- I stood up and the poem [INAUDIBLE].
[NON-ENGLISH].
I'm getting Yiddish and the Polish mixed up.
And I started reciting, and it got so quiet in that class.
I guess I put an [INAUDIBLE].
I tell my students, you have to put it with feeling.
And I guess I did it with feelings.
The kids started applauding.
And that's-- then I became a kind a butterfly that all of a sudden, I started flying, you know?
And I started-- in school, I liked when they gave us assignments, but you could choose the topic.
And we did a lot of that.
It wasn't, you know, you have to write on this topic, do research, or whatever.
Express your thoughts.
Pick a topic.
And I remember in Yiddish I wrote an essay called [YIDDISH], 'What the Doll Is Telling Me.' My mother had made a doll out of pieces of fabric.
We couldn't afford it, those big fancy dolls that they had in stores.
So she made a cloth doll out of fabric.
And what the doll is telling me-- what did the doll tell me?
How hard my mother's working.
How much love she puts in.
And I guess it was a good essay because the teacher made me go around read it to the other students.
Of course, everybody spoke Yiddish.
And this became the beginning of my creativity because they-- everybody seemed to have-- seemed to like it.
And I felt that I could express something, and I started writing.
How old were you when you first started to write like that?
10 Years old.
My uncle was a teacher there, and-- my mother's brother-- and of course, he knew everything that was going on-- the good and the bad.
[LAUGHS].
Funny thing about school too-- I was supposed to wear glasses.
But you know, girls at that age, glasses, it wasn't right.
So I didn't wear the glasses.
But I came to school, and I didn't tell the teacher that I don't see the blackboard.
I waited.
When class was finished, I would run over to the border and quickly write down what was on the board.
So she didn't know that I can't see.
And one day we had an inspector, and the inspectors from the Board of Education, they were very strict.
They wanted-- they were looking for excuses to close the Jewish schools.
So the inspector came, wanted to see, you know, how well those children are doing at that school.
So she called-- you want to show off with your good students, you know?
So one of the teachers-- it was Polish class, Mrs.
Yoskowitz. ?] And she says to me-- it's funny.
My Yiddish name is Riva, or Rivkala, basically.
The Polish version was [?
Reginka, ?] like you would put English.
And so, [?
Reginka, ?] can you tell me what's on the picture.
There was a picture on the wall.
I couldn't see the picture, but I wouldn't say that I can't see the picture.
So I just made up a story.
And I was telling her what the boat-- where the boat is going.
[LAUGHS] And everybody started laughing.
And I-- what are they laughing?
I didn't know what they were laughing.
And him, he laughed.
The teacher laughed.
Then when they left the classroom, I climbed on the desk, looked up.
It was a bowl of fruit.
But I made out a most interesting story.
[LAUGHS] So these are the experiences that, you know, kind of stuck in, in my mind.
The school wasn't closed up, so I guess we passed the test.
Till the war-- till the beginning of the war, 1939.
In the ghetto, we still had for a while-- we had some classes, only for a few months.
And then all the teachers were taken away.

Most of them were taken away before that or they escaped to Russia or whatever they went because the educators were the first ones to be taken away-- educators, clergy of all faiths, political leaders.
So we were left without teachers.
So in the beginning, we still had a little bit in the ghetto.
Not right at the beginning-- a little bit later.
They had opened schools for a while, and they even had high school level, college level.
It didn't last too long because everybody had to go to factories and, and start working.
That was that.
Did you recall any anti-Semitism when you were a school child?
Oh, yes.
At the school directly, we didn't.
We went to, to Jewish school, so there was no anti-Semitism there.
But in the streets, first of all, the stores always would have signs, don't buy by the Jew.
Or a Jew go to Palestine-- to Palestine.
[NON-ENGLISH], Jew go to Palestine.
We would hear people being beaten up.
Sometimes we could see-- if somebody went by, you know, if we-- most of the times we stayed-- lived in areas that was basically Jewish, but to go to school, I had to go into different areas.
And if you saw some hooligans, as we called them, you stayed out of the way.
You would go off the sidewalk.
If not, they'll push you off the sidewalk.
We felt the anti-Semitism, yeah.
I don't know exactly.
Well, we're going to school.
We started at age six, so I think we came, came across it one way or another because school starts in Poland early-- age six you-- actually, here we start at age six too now, yeah.
No.
No.
Like sometimes, they would go someplace and say, you know, they pushed us off the sidewalk, the same things, you know?
[NON-ENGLISH], which means an insult, Jew.
Going into a store and they would shove him.
I remember my uncle-- the one that became a teacher. when he went to the university and he was heartbroken because he had to sit on the left side of the room-- in the back on the left side.
Jewish students were not allowed to certain-- in certain seats.
And that kind of bothered him, that's why he didn't continue because medical schools, it will be even worse if you get in as a Jew.
So that's why he went to a Jewish teacher seminary and became, became a teacher.
But we knew that if you go to college, you feel the anti-Semitism every day because you have to sit in the back of the room on the left side.
Harry, my best friend.
We lived-- the area that we lived, though it was mostly Jewish, Mrs.
Gruber, the landlady, we knew that she wasn't Jewish.
The only time we knew, when it came Christmas.
When we had-- Harry had a Christmas tree, and I didn't.
But otherwise, you know, she was so involved in our holidays-- her daughter Olga and her grandson.
Olga was divorced and grandson Harry.
He was same age as I was, and he was my best friend.
And we would-- they always celebrated the holidays with us.
And she acted like our grandmother.
If you run in the hallway, don't run too fast.
You're going to break your foot.
Your mother is a widow.
And she-- it was reinforcing.
Like, you know, she really took care on us.
So we knew-- we knew that she-- they weren't Jewish, but it didn't-- they spoke Yiddish.
In fact, Harry used to go with me to Jewish school.
On Sunday-- because school is six days a week.
So I was home on Saturday because it was a Jewish school.
And he was home Sundays.
So he would come.
The teachers would say, he's the best student.
He loved Jewish history.
And well, and later he was brainwashed.
But he was my best friend till age 13.
September 1, 1939 the Nazis conquered to Poland.
They came to Lodz.
And a few months later, the Jews had to-- were put into ghettos.
At that time, that's where Mrs.
Gruber had shown her anti-Semitism.
